comments.text: The following has been checked (a sanity check on the intuition below with data): among the smallest $20000$ primes $p$, there are $1278$ for which $\gcd(2^p-1,3^p-1)>1.$ So for $18722$ of them, we have $h(p)=3.$ Heuristically, the larger $p$ the larger the probability that $h(p)=1.$ This is since $\gcd(2^p-1,3^p-1)>1$ implies there is a prime $q=kp+1$ dividing both. There are fewer values of (even) $k$ for which these are prime (behaves like one over $\log(k)+\log(p)$...), and so fewer chances that both $2$ and $3$ are $k^{th}$ powers modulo $kp+1$. Actually, the density of primes for which $h(p)=3$ should be one. (the probability of failing can be estimated above by $\frac{1}{\log p} \left( 1- \frac{\pi^2}{24}\right)$ for every $p$) Anyone who thinks this can be made rigourous?

Small observation on second problem: we will need to prove that either there are infinitely many primes $n$ such that $h(n)=3$, or all prime powers of a fixed prime satisfy $h(p^k)=3$. (if $h(n)=3$, then every prime divisor $d$ of $n$ satisfies $h(d)=3$ and thus only finitely many prime powers with value $3$ implies also finitely many values in general have value $3$)

General thoughts on the last problem: for fixed $\epsilon>0$, $n$ large and $p<n^\epsilon$ the largest prime such that $p-1 \mid n$. Assume that $2^n-1,3^n-1 \ldots, p^n-1$ are all multiples of a prime $r>p$. Now $2,3,\ldots, p$ are all $k^{th}$ powers modulo $r$, where $k=\frac{r-1}{\gcd(r-1,n)}>2$. If $r \le 2n$, this should be impossible since one expects only $\Theta(\log(n))<n^{\epsilon}$ many consecutive $k^{th}$ powers modulo $r$. (see also the discussion and links at [stackexchange]) If $r>2n$, then $\log_k(r) \le \log_k(kn+1)<\log_2(n+1)+1$ and the same estimate implies we do not expect this to happen. (observation: We can exclude $k$ large, in terms of $\epsilon$, by considering the number of $n^{\epsilon}$ smooth numbers up to $n$.) $\textbf{note about small values}$ for $n=4044, h(n)=17>13$ (largest prime such that $p-1 \mid 4044$) Here $\log_{4044}{13}>0.3$ is reasonable big. (but so we have to think as $n$ to be sufficiently large for every $\epsilon$)

Some thoughts on non-conditional proofs: $\gcd(2^p-1,3^p-1) \not=1$ is possible when $2^p-1$ is not a prime, e.g. when $p=11.$ One may expect on the other hand that the chance that the gcd is not $1$ is somewhat small. A prime factor $kp+1$, where $k$ is even, can only occur if both $2$ and $3$ are perfect $k^{th}$ powers. ($\sum_{ 2 \mid k} 1/k^2 <1$). Proving it for prime powers also looked an option. E.g., if $n=5^k$, then $\gcd(2^n-1,3^n-1)=1$ whenever $k\le 10.$ Nevertheless, this fails for $k \ge 42$, since in that case $72*5^{42}+1$ is a divisor of both $2^n-1$ and $3^n-1$. So there is likely no argument why it should work for all prime powers of another prime, e.g. $n=3^k$ for all k. Showing that a certain pattern/ infinite subset among the many solutions exists, see [oeis/A263647] for the first $10000$ values, may be hard or even impossible.

For the second question: so it is conjectured that $h(n)=3$ infinitely often. Conditional on the assumption that there are infinitely many Mersenne primes, which seems to be widely believed meanwhile (see e.g. [wiki:Lenstra-Pomerance-Wagstaff] and links for some conjecture on the counting function), this can be confirmed. Let $q=2^p-1$ be a Mersenne prime. Here $q=kp+1$ for some integer $k$. We have that $q \mid 3^p-1$ if $3$ is a $k^{th}$ power modulo $q.$ But the $k^{th}$ powers modulo $q$ are exactly $1,2,2^2,2^3, \ldots,2^{p-1}$. So $3$ is not among them. We conclude that $\gcd(2^p-1,3^p-1)=1.$

Guess it is important to connect this problem to [\#820]. In particular there is the connection to the OEIS there, and a link to the conjecture that says $h(n)=3$ occurs infinitely often, see [Ailon-Rudnick]. For the last question; guess this has to be interpreted for any $\epsilon>0$, since I expect that it is true for $\epsilon>1/3$.
